section.title: The Second Journey: Philippi
section.sectionRationale: The second missionary journey begins in tension: Paul and Barnabas part ways over Mark, and Paul sets out with Silas. Timothy joins at Lystra, then the Spirit closes door after door until, through a night vision, He calls the team across the sea into Europe. At Philippi the gospel takes root in three unlikely lives: a wealthy businesswoman, an exploited slave girl, and a hardened Roman jailer, showing that the risen Christ gathers a people from every rank.
section.thesis: The ascended Christ, by His Spirit, steers His messengers into Europe and there builds His church out of the most unlikely people, opening a rich woman's heart, freeing a slave girl, and saving a jailer and his household, so that the way of salvation is preached and believed at the heart of a Roman colony.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the sharp parting with Barnabas over Mark; Timothy joining the team; the Spirit's closed doors and the Macedonian call; Lydia's conversion by the river; the freeing of the slave girl and the imprisonment; and the midnight earthquake and the jailer's conversion and release.

verses.text: Some time later Paul said to Barnabas, “Let us go back and visit the brothers in every town where we proclaimed the word of the Lord, to see how they are doing.”
Barnabas wanted to take John, also called Mark.
But Paul thought it best not to take him, because he had deserted them in Pamphylia and had not accompanied them in the work.
Their {{paroxysmos|disagreement}} was so sharp that they parted company. Barnabas took Mark and sailed for Cyprus,
but Paul chose Silas and left, commended by the brothers to the grace of the Lord.
And he traveled through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the churches.
Paul came to Derbe and then to Lystra, where he found a disciple named Timothy, the son of a believing Jewish woman and a Greek father.
The brothers in Lystra and Iconium spoke well of him.
Paul wanted Timothy to accompany him, so he took him and circumcised him on account of the Jews in that area, for they all knew that his father was a Greek.
As they went from town to town, they delivered the decisions handed down by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem for the people to obey.
So the churches were strengthened in the faith and grew daily in numbers.

units.label: The Macedonian call
units.note: the Spirit closes doors, then opens the sea

verses.text: After the Holy Spirit had prevented them from speaking the word in the province of Asia, they traveled through the region of Phrygia and Galatia.
And when they came to the border of Mysia, they tried to enter Bithynia, but the Spirit of Jesus would not permit them.
So they passed by Mysia and went down to Troas.
During the night, Paul had a vision of a man of Macedonia standing and pleading with him, “Come over to Macedonia and help us.”
As soon as Paul had seen the vision, we got ready to leave for Macedonia, concluding that God had called us to preach the gospel to them.
We sailed from Troas straight to Samothrace, and the following day on to Neapolis.
From there we went to the Roman colony of Philippi, the leading city of that district of Macedonia. And we stayed there several days.
On the Sabbath we went outside the city gate along the river, where it was customary to find a place of prayer. After sitting down, we spoke to the women who had gathered there.
Among those listening was a woman named Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira, who was a worshiper of God. The Lord {{dianoigo|opened}} her heart to respond to Paul’s message.
And when she and her household had been baptized, she urged us, “If you consider me a believer in the Lord, come and stay at my house.” And she persuaded us.

units.label: The slave girl and the prison
units.note: a spirit cast out, and a beating

verses.text: One day as we were going to the place of prayer, we were met by a slave girl with a spirit of divination, who earned a large income for her masters by fortune-telling.
This girl followed Paul and the rest of us, shouting, “These men are servants of the Most High God, who are proclaiming to you the way of salvation!”

She continued this for many days. Eventually Paul grew so aggravated that he turned and said to the spirit, “In the name of Jesus Christ I command you to come out of her!” And the spirit left her at that very moment.
When the girl’s owners saw that their hope of making money was gone, they seized Paul and Silas and dragged them before the authorities in the marketplace.
They brought them to the magistrates and said, “These men are Jews and are throwing our city into turmoil
by promoting customs that are unlawful for us Romans to adopt or practice.”
The crowd joined in the attack against Paul and Silas, and the magistrates ordered that they be stripped and beaten with rods.
And after striking them with many blows, they threw them into prison and ordered the jailer to guard them securely.
On receiving this order, he placed them in the inner cell and fastened their feet in the stocks.

units.label: The jailer's conversion and release
units.note: believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved

verses.text: About midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the other prisoners were listening to them.
Suddenly a strong earthquake shook the foundations of the prison. At once all the doors flew open and everyone’s chains came loose.
When the jailer woke up and saw the prison doors open, he drew his sword and was about to kill himself, presuming that the prisoners had escaped.
But Paul called out in a loud voice, “Do not harm yourself! We are all here!”
Calling for lights, the jailer rushed in and fell trembling before Paul and Silas.
Then he brought them out and asked, “Sirs, what must I do to be saved?”
They replied, “{{pisteuo|Believe}} in the Lord Jesus and you will be {{sozo|saved}}, you and your household.”
Then Paul and Silas spoke the word of the Lord to him and to everyone in his house.
At that hour of the night, the jailer took them and washed their wounds. And without delay, he and all his household were baptized.
Then he brought them into his home and set a meal before them. So he and all his household rejoiced that they had come to believe in God.
When daylight came, the magistrates sent their officers with the order: “Release those men.”
The jailer informed Paul: “The magistrates have sent orders to release you. Now you may go on your way in peace.”

But Paul said to the officers, “They beat us publicly without a trial and threw us into prison, even though we are Roman citizens. And now do they want to send us away secretly? Absolutely not! Let them come themselves and escort us out!”
So the officers relayed this message to the magistrates, who were alarmed to hear that Paul and Silas were Roman citizens.
They came to appease them and led them out, requesting that they leave the city.
After Paul and Silas came out of the prison, they went to Lydia’s house to see the brothers and encourage them. Then they left the city.

paroxysmos.gloss: A sharp provocation, an irritation that flares into contention. The word gives us paroxysm. Luke does not hide it: two godly men had so heated a clash over Mark that they parted. Yet God overruled the friction, and two mission teams went out instead of one; the same word is later used positively for spurring one another on to love, Hebrews 10:24.

dianoigo.gloss: To open thoroughly, to open what was shut. It is used of Jesus opening the Scriptures and opening minds on the Emmaus road, Luke 24:32, 45. Here the Lord opens Lydia's heart to respond, so that her faith is plainly God's gift; Paul preaches, but God grants the hearing that saves.

pisteuo.gloss: To believe, to trust, to rest one's confidence upon. To the jailer's cry, what must I do to be saved, the answer is not a program but a person: believe in the Lord Jesus. Faith is the empty hand that receives salvation, and it reached his whole household as they heard the word.

sozo.gloss: To save, rescue, make whole. The slave girl's spirit had cried the way of salvation, and now the jailer asks how to be saved. The verb spans rescue from danger and rescue from sin and death; here it is the full salvation given to all who believe in the Lord Jesus.

basic.context: The second journey opens with a painful split: Paul and Barnabas disagree so sharply over taking Mark that they part company. Paul takes Silas, adds Timothy at Lystra, and heads west. Then the Spirit blocks their plans until a night vision calls them to Macedonia, and the gospel crosses into Europe. At Philippi, a Roman colony, three very different people are saved: Lydia the businesswoman, a slave girl set free from a spirit, and the jailer who nearly took his own life.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as one connected story of how Christ builds His church. The key question is who is really in charge of the mission. Notice how often God acts: the Spirit forbids, the Spirit of Jesus stops them, God calls through a vision, the Lord opens Lydia's heart. Human plans, and even human quarrels, are woven into God's purpose to bring the gospel to Philippi.
basic.originalLanguages: The clash with Barnabas is a paroxysmos, a sharp flare of contention, yet God brings good from it. When Lydia hears, the Lord dianoigo, opens, her heart, the same word for Jesus opening the Scriptures. And to the jailer's question the answer is pisteuo, believe, and you will be sozo, saved, faith resting on the Lord Jesus for full rescue.
basic.typology: Paul and Silas singing at midnight, then an earthquake that flings the doors open and looses every chain, pictures the freedom the gospel brings; captives set free while their jailer is the one truly bound by fear. Lydia's baptism and the jailer's household baptism show the covenant sign now marking those who believe in Christ.
basic.christ: The whole advance is directed by the Spirit of Jesus, the ascended Lord governing His mission. It is His name that casts out the spirit of divination, and it is faith in the Lord Jesus that saves the jailer and his house. Christ is the content of the message, the power behind the miracles, and the Saviour those who hear come to trust.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the mission and gracious to sinners. He closes doors and opens hearts. He does not wait for the respectable; He saves a wealthy woman, a trafficked girl, and a rough jailer alike, showing that salvation is by grace through faith and open to all ranks and races.
basic.crossReferences: Philippians was later written to this very church, remembering how the gospel first came. Luke 24 shows the same opening of hearts and Scriptures. Romans 10 declares that faith comes by hearing the word of Christ, just as Lydia and the jailer believed on hearing.
basic.oneStory: This is the gospel reaching a new continent. The risen Christ, by His Spirit, carries His word from Asia into Europe and plants a church at Philippi out of the most unlikely people. The one story of Scripture, God gathering a people for Himself from every nation, takes a decisive step west toward the ends of the earth.

context.literary: A hinge in Acts. Luke marks the second journey and, at verse 10 of chapter 16, quietly shifts to we, signalling that the narrator has joined the team. The Philippi account is told in three vivid conversion scenes framed by the Macedonian call and the official climbdown before Roman citizens.
context.historical: Philippi was a Roman colony proud of its citizenship, which explains the charge that Paul promotes unlawful customs and the alarm when the magistrates learn they have beaten Roman citizens. Lydia's trade in purple marks her as prosperous; the slave girl's owners profit from her exploitation; the jailer is a working Roman official. The scene captures a cross section of colonial society.
context.canonical: The founding of the Philippian church, later addressed in Paul's warmest letter. It shows the pattern of Acts, the gospel moving from Jerusalem outward, now crossing into Europe, and it models conversion as God's initiative met by faith and sealed in baptism.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the Lord Jesus directs the spread of the gospel by His Spirit, and that He saves people of every kind by grace through faith, establishing His church even through opposition, imprisonment, and the messengers' own weaknesses.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Much here is descriptive: the specific route, the vision, the earthquake, Timothy's circumcision for the sake of mission among Jews. The abiding truths are prescriptive: salvation is God's gift received by faith in the Lord Jesus, and the church is built by the Spirit's leading, not merely human strategy.

debates.question: Why did Paul circumcise Timothy so soon after the Jerusalem council freed Gentiles from circumcision?

views.label: A missionary accommodation, not a matter of salvation
views.case: Timothy had a Jewish mother, so circumcision removed a needless obstacle to reaching Jews; Paul acted to become all things to all men, 1 Corinthians 9:20, without conceding that circumcision saves. This fits Acts 15, where the issue was whether Gentiles must be circumcised to be saved.

views.label: An inconsistency with the council's ruling
views.case: Some see tension with Galatians, where Paul refused to circumcise Titus. Rightly weighed, the cases differ: Titus was a Gentile and the gospel itself was at stake, while Timothy was reckoned a Jew and the concern was freedom for mission, so there is no compromise of principle.

debates.bearing: The distinction guards the gospel: circumcision cannot save, yet believers may freely limit their liberty for the sake of reaching others, so long as they never make a work a condition of grace.

study.originalLanguages: The section turns on a few telling words. The rift with Barnabas is a paroxysmos, a sharp contention, that God nonetheless uses. At Philippi the Lord dianoigo, opens wide, Lydia's heart, the same verb Luke used of Jesus opening minds to Scripture, so that her faith is manifestly God's work. And the jailer's midnight cry meets the gospel in its simplest form, pisteuo, believe, on the Lord Jesus and you will be sozo, saved, the salvation the slave girl's spirit had unwillingly announced.

typology.fulfillment: The gospel that frees captives while the jailer, bound by fear, is himself set free by faith, a picture of Christ proclaiming liberty to the captives, Luke 4:18.
The new covenant work of God writing His word on the heart, Jeremiah 31:33, fulfilled as the Lord opens hearts to believe the gospel of Christ.
The covenant sign, once circumcision, now baptism marking those who by grace believe in the Lord Jesus, Colossians 2:11-12.

study.christ: The risen Christ is the unseen leader of this mission. It is the Spirit of Jesus who redirects the team toward Europe, His name that casts out the spirit of divination, and faith in Him that saves the jailer and his household. The message preached is the word of the Lord, and the salvation offered is His own. Even the messengers' chains become an occasion for Christ to be glorified, as praying and singing in the dark give way to an earthquake of grace.
study.god: God rules the mission and rescues sinners. He closes the door to Asia and Bithynia and opens the way to Macedonia; He opens Lydia's heart; He shakes the prison and saves the jailer. He shows no partiality, gathering rich and poor, free and enslaved, Jew and Roman into one people. His sovereignty does not cancel human responsibility but secures the fruit of the preached word, so that all the glory of the harvest is His.

crossReferences.note: Paul later thanks God for this church's partnership in the gospel from the first day, the fruit of the founding told here.
Jesus opened their minds to understand the Scriptures, the same divine opening seen in Lydia's heart.
Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, and faith comes by hearing the word of Christ, as at Philippi.
Paul becomes all things to all men to save some, explaining the circumcision of Timothy for the mission.

study.oneStory: The gospel crosses into Europe. The ascended Christ, steering His messengers by the Spirit, plants His church at Philippi out of a businesswoman, a slave girl, and a jailer, proof that salvation is by grace through faith and belongs to every kind of person. In the sweep of Scripture this is another stride of the one mission, God gathering a people from every nation until the word reaches the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: What redirected Paul's team toward Macedonia?

options.text: The Spirit closed other doors, and then God called them through a night vision.
options.note: Luke stresses the Spirit forbidding Asia and Bithynia, then the Macedonian vision, so God directed the move.

options.text: Paul had a strategic plan to reach the major cities of Europe.
options.note: Paul did aim at strategic centres, but the text credits the Spirit's leading and the vision, not merely his planning.

options.text: They were fleeing persecution and simply went the nearest way.
options.note: They faced opposition elsewhere, but the move to Macedonia is presented as God's guidance, not flight.

options.text: The Jerusalem apostles ordered them to evangelize Europe.
options.note: No such order is given; the direction comes from the Spirit and the vision.

quiz.q: How did Lydia come to believe?

options.text: The Lord opened her heart to respond to Paul's message.
options.note: Luke says plainly the Lord opened her heart, making her faith God's gift.

options.text: She heard Paul's message and responded to it.
options.note: True, she did respond to the preached word, but the decisive cause named is the Lord opening her heart.

options.text: She was already a worshiper of God, so she needed no conversion.
options.note: She was a worshiper of God, yet she still needed her heart opened to believe the gospel of Christ.

options.text: She was persuaded by the miracle of the slave girl.
options.note: Her conversion precedes that event and is credited to the Lord opening her heart, not a miracle.

quiz.q: What answer did Paul and Silas give the jailer's question, what must I do to be saved?

options.text: Believe in the Lord Jesus and you will be saved, you and your household.
options.note: This is their exact reply: salvation by faith in the Lord Jesus.

options.text: Be baptized and wash our wounds as a sign of repentance.
options.note: He was baptized and did wash their wounds, but these followed faith; the saving instruction was to believe.

options.text: Release us and make amends for the beating.
options.note: He did release and care for them, yet that was not the answer to how to be saved.

options.text: Keep the customs of Moses and be circumcised.
options.note: That is the very error the Jerusalem council rejected; the answer was faith in Christ.

quiz.q: What does the parting of Paul and Barnabas over Mark show?

options.text: Even godly leaders can clash sharply, yet God can bring good out of it.
options.note: Luke records the sharp contention honestly, and two teams went out where there had been one.

options.text: The disagreement was sinful on both sides and hindered the mission.
options.note: There was real fault in the heat of it, but Luke frames the outcome as God overruling for wider mission, not simple failure.

options.text: Barnabas abandoned the gospel by siding with Mark.
options.note: Barnabas did not abandon the gospel; he took Mark to Cyprus, and Mark was later useful again.

options.text: Paul was proven wrong, since Mark never served usefully again.
options.note: Mark was later called useful for ministry, 2 Timothy 4:11, so this is false.

quiz.q: Who were the three people converted at Philippi, and what do they show together?

options.text: Lydia, the slave girl, and the jailer, showing Christ saves every rank and race.
options.note: A wealthy woman, an exploited girl, and a Roman jailer show the reach of grace across society.

options.text: A group of well-to-do citizens who formed the new church.
options.note: Lydia was prosperous, but the point is the mix of ranks, not a single class of people.

options.text: Only Jews who already feared God and joined the synagogue.
options.note: Lydia was a God-fearer, but the jailer was a pagan Roman, so this misses the breadth.

options.text: Roman magistrates who repented after the earthquake.
options.note: The magistrates were alarmed but not converted; the three saved were Lydia, the slave girl, and the jailer.

questions: Paul and Barnabas parted over a sharp disagreement, yet God still advanced the mission. Where is a strained relationship tempting you to give up on God's work, and how might He still be at work through it?
The Spirit closed doors before He opened the right one. How do you respond when your plans are blocked, and can you trust that God may be redirecting rather than abandoning you?
The Lord opened Lydia's heart to believe. Whose heart are you longing to see opened, and how does it free you to pray when you remember that faith is God's gift?
Paul and Silas sang hymns at midnight in the stocks. What would it look like for you to worship God in the middle of your own hardship this week?
The gospel reached a rich woman, a trafficked girl, and a rough jailer alike. Who might you be quietly writing off as beyond reach, and how does Philippi challenge that?

translationNotes.note: The province of Asia renders literally in Asia, a Roman province in what is now western Turkey, not the continent. Footnote markers in the source were omitted when building the verse text.

The description of Philippi as the leading city of that district reflects a textual and translation choice; the source footnote notes an alternative rendering. The meaning that Philippi was a prominent Roman colony is unaffected.

A spirit of divination translates literally a spirit of Python, a spirit of soothsaying named after the mythical serpent slain by Apollo, indicating an occult, demonic source behind the girl's fortune-telling.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 15 and 16. Verse text built by concatenating string and text elements with single spaces; footnote and lineBreak objects skipped. Acts 15:34 is absent from the BSB and falls outside this passage. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
